claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the eligibility assessment.

The first thing I need to know is about the child (or children) you're claiming for.

**How old is your child?**

Please let me know if they are:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children you'd like to check eligibility for, please tell me each of their ages (and their names would be helpful too so I can keep track).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Oh hello, thanks for helping me with this! I've got three kids actually - Alex, Blake, and Charlie. 

Alex is 5 years old, Blake is 17, and Charlie is 19. They all live with me at home. Does that help?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! That's very helpful, thank you for providing all those details! Let me work through the eligibility for each of your three children - Alex (5), Blake (17), and Charlie (19).

Let me start checking each child's eligibility based on their ages.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Here's what I've found so far:

---

**Alex (5 years old)** ✅ - Great news! Children under 16 automatically qualify based on age. I just have one quick question to confirm eligibility:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

*(I imagine at 5 years old, Alex isn't working, but I need to confirm this for the official assessment!)*

---

For **Blake (17)** and **Charlie (19)**, since they're between 16-19 years old, I need to check their education or training status.

**For both Blake and Charlie, please tell me:**

Are they:
- **Currently in approved education or training** (such as A-levels, T-levels, NVQs, apprenticeships, etc.)?
- **Recently left education** (within the last few months)?
- **Neither** - not in education/training and haven't been recently?

Take your time - you can answer for all three children at once if that's easier!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Oh right, yes of course! Alex is only 5, so no, he's definitely not working - he's at primary school!

As for Blake and Charlie - Blake actually left school and isn't doing anything at the moment, so neither of those options I suppose.

But Charlie is still studying - she's doing her A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying all of that! Let me update the assessment for each child.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is really helpful. Let me update you on where we are:

---

**Alex (5 years old)** ✅ - Confirmed not working, so we're progressing nicely with Alex.

---

**Charlie (19, doing A-levels)** - Great news! A-levels are on the approved list of qualifications. I just need to confirm a couple of things:

1. **Is Charlie studying full-time (at least 12 hours per week of supervised study)?**
2. **Is Charlie working less than 24 hours per week (or not working at all)?**

---

**Blake (17, left school, not doing anything)** - Since Blake has left education, there *might* be an extension period available. Let me ask:

1. **How recently did Blake leave school/education?** (within the last 20 weeks, or longer ago?)
2. **Has Blake registered with any of these services?**
   - A government careers service
   - Connexions
   - The armed services

The 20-week extension can help maintain eligibility for young people who have recently left education and are registered with a qualifying service.

---

Could you let me know the answers for Charlie and Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Oh yes, Charlie is definitely full-time at college - she's there Monday to Friday doing her coursework and classes. And no, she doesn't have a job, she's focused on her studies.

As for Blake, well, he left school maybe a couple of months ago? And yes, actually, he did sign up with the local careers service to try and figure out what he wants to do next. They've been helping him look at options.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's really helpful, thank you! Let me process this information for both Charlie and Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good progress! Now I have a few more quick questions to complete the assessment for all three children.

---

**For all three children (Alex, Blake, and Charlie):**

1. **Are any of them receiving benefits in their own right?** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit claimed by them personally - not benefits you claim for the household)

---

**Also, regarding Blake specifically:**

You mentioned he left school a couple of months ago and registered with the local careers service - that's great news! Since it's been less than 20 weeks and he's registered with a qualifying service, Blake may still be eligible under the **20-week extension period**.

Just to confirm: **Is Blake under 18?** (The extension ends when a young person turns 18, whichever comes first.)

---

Once you confirm these details, I should have everything I need to give you a final answer for all three children!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
No, none of them are receiving any benefits in their own name - they're just my kids living at home with me!

And yes, Blake is 17, so he's still under 18.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news - we're making great progress! Just one more question about the children:

**Are any of the children (Alex, Blake, or Charlie) currently doing an apprenticeship?**

(This includes Intermediate, Advanced, Higher, or Degree Apprenticeships in England, or Modern Apprenticeships in Scotland. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are fine and don't disqualify.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of them are doing apprenticeships. Alex is too young obviously, Blake isn't doing anything right now since he left school, and Charlie is just at college doing her A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to check a few things about you (the claimant).

**Do you meet any of the following residency requirements?**

- **You normally live in the UK**, OR
- You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme, OR
- You have **pre-settled status** and meet certain conditions (e.g., working, studying, or have sufficient resources), OR
- You're a **Crown servant** living abroad

For most people, the first option applies - do you normally live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, Manchester - lovely! ☀️ (well, sometimes! 😄)

Now, I need to confirm your responsibility for the children. You mentioned earlier that all three children live with you at home - is that correct?

**Do you live with Alex, Blake, and Charlie?**

(Alternatively, if any of them don't live with you, you could still qualify if you're contributing to their upkeep - such as paying for food, clothes, housing, pocket money, etc.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - all three of them live here at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
We're nearly there! Just a couple more questions.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, Blake, or Charlie?**

For example, does their other parent or another family member claim Child Benefit for any of them?

(Only one person can receive Child Benefit for each child at a time.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them. It's just me looking after them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Just one more question about the children's living situation.

**Are any of the children (Alex, Blake, or Charlie) currently:**

- **In hospital** for an extended period?
- **In local authority care?**
- **In a fostering arrangement?**

Or are they all just **living normally at home with you** in Manchester?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They're all just living normally at home with me, nothing special like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Almost done! Final question:

**Is the local council paying anything towards the children's accommodation or maintenance?**

(For example, if the council provides financial support specifically for housing or caring for any of the children.)

If not, and you're simply supporting them yourself, that's what we need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything - I'm supporting them all myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Wonderful news! I now have all the information I need to give you a final assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

# 🎉 Great News! You're Eligible for Child Benefit for All Three Children!

Here's the breakdown for each child:

---

## ✅ Alex (age 5)
**ELIGIBLE** - As a child under 16, Alex automatically qualifies. All other criteria are met (living with you, no other claimant, council not paying for maintenance).

---

## ✅ Blake (age 17)
**ELIGIBLE** - Although Blake has left school, he qualifies under the **20-week extension period** because:
- He left education within the last 20 weeks
- He's registered with the local careers service (a qualifying service)
- He's still under 18

⚠️ **Important note for Blake:** This extension period will end either after 20 weeks from when he left education OR when he turns 18, whichever comes first. If Blake starts approved education/training or an approved apprenticeship before then, his eligibility could continue beyond that.

---

## ✅ Charlie (age 19)
**ELIGIBLE** - Charlie is in approved full-time education (A-levels), which qualifies her until she finishes her course or turns 20, whichever comes first.

---

## 📝 Next Steps to Claim

1. **Apply online** at [gov.uk](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim) or by post using **form CH2**
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and the children
3. **Provide birth certificates** for each child

## 💷 Payment Information
- Payments are made **every 4 weeks** (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

## ⚠️ High Income Child Benefit Charge
If you or a partner earn over **£60,000** per year, you may need to pay back some or all of the Child Benefit through the High Income Child Benefit Charge. You can still claim, but it's worth checking if this applies to you.

---

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or the application process?

reasonings: Alex is 5 years old (under 16), which means he automatically qualifies based on age. He is not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, lives with the claimant, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him, and the council is not paying for his maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

Blake is 17 years old and has left school. However, he qualifies under the 20-week extension period because: (1) he left education within the last 20 weeks (a couple of months ago), (2) he has registered with a qualifying service (local careers service), and (3) he is still under 18. He is not working 24+ hours per week, not receiving benefits in his own right, not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, lives with the claimant, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him, and the council is not paying for his maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met under the extension rules.

Charlie is 19 years old and is in approved full-time education (A-levels at sixth form college). A-levels are an approved non-advanced qualification, and she is studying full-time (Monday to Friday). She is not working, not receiving benefits in her own right, not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, lives with the claimant, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for her, and the council is not paying for her maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for all three children. They live in the UK (Manchester), all three children live with them at home, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for the children, and the council is not paying for their maintenance. Each child meets the specific age and education/training requirements: Alex qualifies as under 16, Blake qualifies under the 20-week extension period for those who have recently left education and registered with a careers service, and Charlie qualifies as a 16-19 year old in approved full-time education.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Has the child (aged 16-17) left education within the last 20 weeks AND registered with a qualifying service?
response.help_text: 20-week extension available if child has left approved education/training and registered with: government careers service, Connexions, or armed services. Extension ends at week 20 or when child turns 18, whichever is sooner.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

He is not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, lives with the claimant, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him, and the council is not paying for his maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

Blake is 17 years old and has left school. However, he qualifies under the 20-week extension period because: (1) he left education within the last 20 weeks (a couple of months ago), (2) he has registered with a qualifying service (local careers service), and (3) he is still under 18. He is not working 24+ hours per week, not receiving benefits in his own right, not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, lives with the claimant, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him, and the council is not paying for his maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met under the extension rules.

Charlie is 19 years old and is in approved full-time education (A-levels at sixth form college). A-levels are an approved non-advanced qualification, and she is studying full-time (Monday to Friday). She is not working, not receiving benefits in her own right, not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, lives with the claimant, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for her, and the council is not paying for her maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

They live in the UK (Manchester), all three children live with them at home, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for the children, and the council is not paying for their maintenance. Each child meets the specific age and education/training requirements: Alex qualifies as under 16, Blake qualifies under the 20-week extension period for those who have recently left education and registered with a careers service, and Charlie qualifies as a 16-19 year old in approved full-time education.
